Interesting report from @theinformation that Nvidia will be able to make 1000 Vera Rubin racks per day, which is $630b per quarter. Actually a little hard for me to believe and haven’t checked the math, but wow if true.

And Vera CPU racks and Groq LPU racks would be incremental to this.

As would their new business model where they take a revenue share of neocloud revenue in return for guaranteeing offtake.

I do think the latter point has not been well explored by analysts and is likely to be super important going forward.

tweets.full_text:
Interesting report from @theinformation that Nvidia will be able to make 1000 Vera Rubin racks per day, which is $630b per quarter. Actually a little hard for me to believe and haven’t checked the math, but wow if true.

And Vera CPU racks and Groq LPU racks would be incremental to this.

As would their new business model where they take a revenue share of neocloud revenue in return for guaranteeing offtake.

I do think the latter point has not been well explored by analysts and is likely to be super important going forward.

And obviously the $630b would be at the system level and not all go to Nvidia unless they change their business model to lower their margins.

Also can’t get over the idea that Nvidia is the leading supporter of open source AI and many people seem to believe that open source AI models would be negative for AI infrastructure demand and Nvidia.

Good luck to everyone who is using Nvidia as a “safe” funding short if any of this is remotely true.

tweets.text:
"The fuel everyone thinks is abundant is not."

Matthew's letter shows storage capacity grew 7% since 2010 while demand grew 54%, with another 39% coming by 2030. 

He forecasts an unprecedented gas shortage starting in 2028, and explains why the market won't see it coming:

"Natural gas is well supplied today, '26 and '27, and the result is that nobody is investing in gas. 

EQT is shutting in natural gas right now because they think it'll be more valuable later.

The things that we can see real time to figure out if the market is onto this tightness — no one's onto it. 

It's perpetuating this view that most Americans have, which is there's plenty of natural gas, because for 15 years it's all we've been told.

There's a complacency that's developed, and we think that complacency is gonna take us right up to the point where it's too late. 

Gas has lulled everybody to sleep.

In '28, the curve is flat because people believe the gas is abundant. That's despite all these AI compute announcements.

We start to draw meaningfully in the middle of '28 on the gas system like we've never drawn before. By 2029, we drop below all known historical storage evidence.

Prices have gone to $6 or $8 or $10, but those have been transitory. 

What we're talking about are structural drivers of demand against a known possible production of gas, and they don't match up.

The deficit gets really convex and unbounded."

tweets.text:
Matthew Smith has spent the last 18 months modeling every well, pipeline, storage facility, and power plant in the American natural gas system.

His conclusion is that the US is heading toward a natural gas shortage with no precedent, beginning in 2028.

By 2030, he believes we could exhaust our working natural gas storage entirely.

The fuel everyone in AI is counting on, and that everyone assumes is abundant, is not there.

And because gas sets the price of electricity in most of the country, he argues Americans will pay for the shortage in their power bills.

Matthew has worked in energy markets for over 20 years and is the CIO of Chronometer Partners. 

This is his second time on the show, and he's one of my favorite people to talk to about energy.

We discuss:
- Why the bottleneck is moving from power to fuel
- Why we can't just shut off exports
- 2028 as the inflection point
- Large-scale nuclear v. SMRs
- Who wins, who loses, and what can still be done

Enjoy!

TIMESTAMPS
0:00 Intro
1:30 What Drives the Deficit
11:00 Why Supply Can’t Catch Up
20:35 The 2030 Gas Crisis
25:05 Winners and Losers
29:00 Nuclear and Solar
33:30 Consumers Pay the Bill
37:20 AI’s Next Shortage
45:25 Solutions and Global Stakes
51:15 The Coming Gas Knife Fight

tweets.text:
The global helium supply chain is tightening:

China's helium imports from Qatar have collapsed to near zero, from a peak of ~65 million cubic feet in late-2025, the lowest on record.

Imports from Russia have declined -50 million cubic feet over the last 2 quarters, to ~38.5 million cubic feet, the lowest since Q3 2025.

The drop comes as the Iran War disrupts exports from Qatar, which supplied ~33% of global helium before the conflict,

As a result, spot helium prices for immediate delivery have roughly DOUBLED over the last 4 months.

Meanwhile, China has introduced new export controls on helium, despite not being a major producer itself, as the country relies heavily on imports to supply critical industries, including semiconductor manufacturing and medical equipment.

China has also been an important transit route for Russian helium into Europe since direct EU imports from Russia were restricted.

Helium supply is being squeezed from multiple directions.

tweets.text:
Important clarification, as I keep seeing misinformation about this:

The $1.5B Anthropic settlement was for pirating books. It was not for *training* on books. The judge ruled it was perfectly fine to train on copyrighted materials as long as you actually bought them.

Now how does this compare to distillation? It's very unclear that distillation, which involves training on reasoning traces that you buy from a third party, is anything like this.

1) Pure AI-generated outputs are not copyrightable, according to the US Copyright Office. 
2) The ToS with OAI/Ant to not train on the model traces restricts the person who signs the ToS, but it's not clear it stops them from selling their own data, or a third party from buying that data.
3) Traces are not hidden, so hard to claim traces can be treated as trade secrets.

It's very unclear that distillation is illegal. It's like building a Pong knock-off game based on the original Pong. Unless Pong is patented and you violate the patent, it's normally legal and just a part of how businesses learn from each other and compete. 

Now, if they are creating webs of tens of thousands of accounts to distill and directly violating Anthropic's ToS, that's a stronger argument of corporate espionage. But if they're just buying this data arm's length from data brokers, good argument that it's legal to distill.

(Any lawyers feel free to correct me)

Notably, Kimi has not released their weights yet. Supposed to be released next week (waiting for Beijing review?)

And the new Qwen model hasn't even said when their weights will be released. 

Neither country seems to know what game they want to play yet. China is vacillating. A few days ago Xi Jinping was saying that Chinese labs should be releasing their weights. Now suddenly a potential pullback?

Maybe he is realizing: AI is not quite like EVs or solar panels. Very different game theory, and it's a lot harder to control what non-Chinese do with Chinese models. 

The thing is, open weights goes against China's general attitude toward social technology. The great firewall, video game curfews, social surveillance, and a battery of political and ideological tests that Chinese AI applications have to pass to be domestically released. Open weights lets people circumvent that. If only Chinese are accessing the models, maybe you can plausibly still regulate it. Perhaps that's what they're thinking.

But this proposed model obviously doesn't work. The weights will leak and there will be American fine-tunes. So 100% this is not the endgame.

tweets.text:
Starting a business has never been cheaper, and AI could make it cheaper still.

In 2004, Tobi Lutke tried to sell snowboards online and found the tools too clunky and expensive. He built his own instead. Shopify launched in 2006, letting founders reach a global audience for roughly $1,000 a year.

Since then, Shopify has bundled payments, logistics, and capital into one platform. About 70% of Shopify's Gross Merchandise Value now runs through Shopify Payments, pooling volume to bring merchants rates once reserved for large retailers.

Shopify Capital loans range from $200 to $2 million, a spread traditional banks likely cannot serve profitably. Originations more than doubled over the two years ended 2025, even as Small Business Administration (SBA) lending fell roughly 15%.

ARK's research suggests AI is now compressing a third cost: labor. Shopify's Sidekick agent functions like an always-on CFO or COO. On average serial entrepreneurs already generate more than 2x the sales of first-time founders after their first sale.

Like any rough estimate there are things that could understate or overstate it, but it's probably in the right order of magnitude.  Here are a few that come to mind:

Things that could raise the cost / job:
- Most manufacturing growth lately has been in high-tech sectors where there are notable tariff waivers which suggests a lesser benefit from tariffs.
- Manufacturing job improvement fully attributed to tariffs may have improved otherwise as the economy continued to grow.

Those that could lower the cost / job:
- The shift in trajectory may be more long lasting which could understate the total job gains that eventually come from the previously implemented surge in tariffs.
- Tariff refunds could make it less costly, but its not clear taxpayers will see that money and most of the job shift happened before the rebates.
- If anything job growth has been better *after* SCOTUS decision which means *lower* effective tariffs are *better* for job creation, not the opposite.

tweets.text:
Not long ago, @IOHK_Charles called the @realfi_co testnet a "big TVL and TX generator for Cardano."

RealFi's own pitch reaches further: give people access to financial services without leaning on traditional banks. That gap between what Charles claimed and what RealFi promises is what pulled me in.

I've been sleeping on Cardano for a while, so I decided to dig into this properly.

1️⃣ What is RealFi?

RealFi is building a stablecoin ecosystem on Cardano designed to connect on-chain capital to real-world credit markets.
Its main stablecoin, USDr, is designed to be backed by real-world assets such as tokenized US Treasuries, money-market funds, public credit, and direct loans to businesses.

2️⃣ What does it do?

RealFi plans to spread the capital backing USDr across liquid financial assets and different forms of credit. Their direct loans can run around 6 to 24 months and focus on companies in payments, digital lending, asset financing, and renewable energy.

So instead of leaning on token emissions or crypto trading strategies, the goal is to generate returns from traditional financial assets and real borrowers.

Users can hold USDr as the liquid stablecoin or stake it to receive sUSDr and earn part of the income generated by that portfolio.

3️⃣ The two-token structure

USDr stays the liquid side and holds close to $1, while sUSDr is the return-bearing side. RealFi's site currently lists up to 10% APY for sUSDr.

If the underlying portfolio earns, that income flows into sUSDr through weekly updates, so each sUSDr should gradually become redeemable for more USDr.

sUSDr also carries more of the risk. The design puts the risk on sUSDr in exchange for the returns, while USDr is meant to stay the stable, liquid side. The exact loss mechanics are worth reading straight from RealFi's own materials before you commit anything.

The way I think about it is simple: someone who mainly wants liquidity stays in USDr, while someone willing to take on more credit risk for higher returns moves into sUSDr.

RealFi also plans to keep around 10–20% of the portfolio in assets that convert to cash quickly, such as tokenized Treasuries and money-market funds.

That gives them a pool for withdrawals, while the rest gets deployed into higher-return assets like public and private credit. So they avoid putting every dollar into longer-term loans and hoping nobody wants to exit.

The two-token idea itself isn't unique, but the way RealFi pairs a liquid stablecoin with a return-bearing layer for a mixed RWA and private-credit portfolio is the part that stands out to me.

One thing worth repeating: private credit carries real credit risk, any committed capital is at risk, and RealFi is unavailable in the US, UK, and EU/EEA.

4️⃣ The testnet

Before real assets and borrower repayments enter the system, RealFi needs to test whether users can move through the full product flow without something breaking. That's where the new testnet comes in.

Phase 1 stress-tests the app, its flows, and integrations, and it's already pulling traffic:
 → 1k+ users joined
 → ~500 active, verified wallets in Phase 1
 → 2k+ new followers here on X
 → 420+ new joiners on Discord

Retail testers can swap test USDCx into USDr, stake USDr for sUSDr, then unstake it again. KYC-verified institutional partners separately test the mint and redemption process for moving larger amounts in and out.

Users also earn R-Points for helping test these flows. R-Points track participation, while RealFi has positioned the future RFG token as governance, meant to give participants a voice in key protocol decisions.

So the flywheel is clear: R-Points drive participation, users generate more transactions, and those transactions give RealFi more chances to catch failed flows before mainnet.

If you'd like to experience it first hand, test it out, here: https://t.co/nOixMX2AsZ

5️⃣ Closing thoughts

I read the two-token structure mainly as a way to separate liquidity from risk. The testnet can show whether that design holds at the product level, but the bigger question is whether RealFi can connect it to real loans and actual economic activity.

That's the real link between the stablecoin and Cardano's old "bank the unbanked" idea.

So I wouldn't say Cardano has delivered on the whole promise yet, but RealFi is at least building something that could move it closer.

Views are my own, none of this is financial advice. Do your own research.

tweets.text:
JTX aims to turn Jito’s infrastructure knowledge into something traders can experience.

Signing a trade does not guarantee good execution. Prices can move and market-maker updates can arrive too late, leaving traders with worse fills.

Jito already influences how Solana transactions are processed through its validator client and Block Engine. 

BAM is the next generation ordering layer pushing this further by keeping transactions private until execution. Its first Maker Priority plugin already helps prop AMMs update quotes earlier in BAM blocks so trades are less likely to hit stale prices.

For spot trading, JTX also utilizes DFlow for its just-in-time routing to deliver the best price on every trade.

If it succeeds, Jito’s reach will extend from Solana’s underlying infrastructure to the interface traders use to access its markets.

It's slightly more nuanced. Basically, $AMD SF event is tomorrow, Wed/Thurs (July 22, 23).

From Jefferies client note, AMD is expected to provide additional disclosures around their likely scale-up CPO roadmap + MI500. 

If they confirm CPO route, would positive thematically since it's not just $NVDA forcing the transition. 

As for $SIVE, we'll get a better read if they're directly in AMD supply chains. 

Since right now, it's possible to map it through $GFS (reportedly involved in AMD MI500 CPO work) or AYAR (which AMD invested in). But no formal confirmations. 

So if you're turning in: 

- CPO thematic positive: MI500 explicitly commits to CPO/optical UALink.

- AMD gives details using Ayar Labs or GF SCALE for MI500 CPO:  That would be extremely positive for $SIVE, given they're the upstream laser supplier / reference laser.  

Currently, Blayne Curtis from Jefferies expect AMD to work with $ALAB on UAL and $AVGO on ESUN... but juicy details are here: 

"We are looking for whether AMD commits to a CPO based scale up solution and who supplies the optical engine"

We'll get a broader read through on the supply chain come Wednesday/Thursday, too early to tell today.

It's slightly more nuanced. Basically, $AMD SF event is tomorrow, Wed/Thurs (July 22, 23).

From Jefferies client note, AMD is expected to provide additional disclosures around their likely scale-up CPO roadmap + MI500. 

If they confirm CPO route, would positive thematically since it's not just $NVDA forcing the transition. 

As for $SIVE, we'll get a better read if they're directly in AMD supply chains. 

Since right now, it's possible to map it through $GFS (reportedly involved in AMD MI500 CPO work) or AYAR (which AMD invested in). But no formal confirmations. 

So if you're turning in: 

- CPO thematic positive: MI500 explicitly commits to CPO/optical UALink.

- AMD gives details using Ayar Labs or GF SCALE for MI500 CPO:  That would be extremely positive for $SIVE, given they're the upstream laser supplier / reference laser.  

Currently, Blayne Curtis from Jefferies expect AMD to work with $ALAB on UAL and $AVGO on ESUN... but juicy details are here: 

"We are looking for whether AMD commits to a CPO based scale up solution and who supplies the optical engine"

We'll get a broader read through on the supply chain come Wednesday/Thursday, too early to tell today.

tweets.text: On this day in 2010, President Obama signed the Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act, the most sweeping overhaul of U.S. financial regulation since the Great Depression. The law was a direct response to the 2008 crisis, which had wiped out Lehman Brothers, forced a $700 billion taxpayer bailout of the banking system, and erased trillions in household wealth. Dodd-Frank created the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, established a council to monitor system-wide risk, imposed the Volcker Rule restricting banks from trading with their own capital, and pushed derivatives onto regulated exchanges. The act ran 848 pages and required more than 390 separate rulemakings across a dozen federal agencies, a build-out that took the better part of a decade to finish. Consumer refunds and relief credited to the bureau it created have since topped $20 billion.

🇨🇳 #China May Have Room to Cut Rates, RRR in 3Q - Shanghai Securities News

➡ China may see a window in easing monetary policy in the third quarter as the government seeks to complement fiscal stimulus measures aimed at supporting the economy, Shanghai Securities News says in a report, citing analysts.

➡ Authorities are expected to ramp up bond sales in the third quarter, and that will likely push the PBOC to inject liquidity into the market to prevent rates from climbing from the pressure of large-scale issuance, Lian Ping, chairman of the China Chief Economists Forum, is cited as saying.

➡ The PBOC may cut interest rates by 10 to 20 basis points, and reduce banks’ reserve requirement ratio by 50 basis points — a move that will inject long-term liquidity into the financial system, according to China Galaxy Securities analyst Zhang Di.

➡ Still, other analysts see reasons for the PBOC to stay put, as weak credit growth means banks have limited need for liquidity, while the economy is still improving in its structure.

*Link (Chinese): https://t.co/3gsC07U3r9

tweets.text:
Most financial products make you pick one:

Lock your asset for yield, or keep it liquid and earn nothing.

Bitcoin has basically lived with that trade-off for years. 

Most holders would rather sit on idle BTC than hand it over just to squeeze out a bit more yield.

Last week, @StackingDao announced stBTC.

The idea is pretty straightforward. BTC earns an expected base yield of around 2.6%, while the stBTC representing that position stays liquid enough to be used across lending and trading apps on Stacks.

BitGo has now added the sBTC bridge, so it’s clients can move between BTC and sBTC without changing the custody platform they’re already using.

BitGo secures around $63B in assets across 5,569 clients and 1.2 million users. That’s a fairly meaningful piece of infrastructure joining the ecosystem.

What I like is how the pieces are starting to connect.

Native BTC yield, a liquid version through stBTC, and custody providers beginning to support the flow instead of sitting outside it.

There are still execution and smart-contract risks once these assets start moving through DeFi.

But if Bitcoin ever develops a real onchain economy, this is probably what it looks like at the beginning.

Pleased to partner with @Stacks to highlight this one.

tweets.text:
AI won’t make you rich.

Not on its own. It’s just leverage.

Multiply your 100x leverage by zero & guess what? You are going to stay at zero.

The people AI has helped make rich were already rich in the things that count.

So here’s why AI won’t make you rich, and what will: 👇

Why it won’t make you rich:

> It’s rented labour. Cheap assistants. Your rival rents the same ones.

> It’s not real leverage vs someone with capital, an audience, and a team.

> It breeds busywork. Most people automate bullshit they should just delete.

> It dodges your bottleneck. Speeding up the wrong thing feels like progress. It isn’t.

> It can’t fix a bad offer. Point it at rubbish and you just reach more people who won’t buy your product.

Alex Hormozi highlighted all of this in a recent video.

So what actually will make you rich?

> Good decisions. One sharp call will beat a thousand different automations.

> One constraint. Find the real bottleneck. Kill it. Ignore the rest.

> Distribution. A mediocre offer people can find beats a great one they can’t. 

> Community. AI can help you write and it can help you build. But it can’t manufacture trust with your bros.

> Fundamentals. Generate demand. You need to deliver something worth buying.

So what moves the needle? Where does AI actually fit in to your plan? 

As we said, it’s leverage, use it as leverage.

Struggling with reach? Take one idea and multiply it.

Sales is the problem? Draft a pitch then personalise it for 500 DMs.

Delivery is the problem? Turn your brain into templates and tools. 

You can sell your time back to yourself.

Got the idea? Use it to start creating it.

AI x (offer + distribution + community) = bags. AI x (busywork + no audience) = broke. 

Same leverage but it’s a different multiplier.

You’re the multiplier bro.

tweets.text:
Bitcoin hasn't fought over its core rules like this since the Blocksize Wars.

There are 2 fights happening at once. 

One's over jpegs and the other's over quantum computers.

Here’s what’s going on:

#1 - BIP-110

This is mainly aimed at Ordinals, Runes, and other data people store inside Bitcoin transactions.

The proposal would temporarily block some of the methods used to jam large amounts of data into blocks. Your $BTC stays safe, existing Ordinals stay where they are, but minting new inscriptions gets way harder for roughly a year.

Here’s the case for it.

Blockspace is limited. So every full node has to download and verify whatever gets added, and supporters think jpegs and tokens are making Bitcoin more expensive to use and harder to run over time.

Where it gets messy is Bitcoin has no idea WHAT the data is. It only sees how the transaction was built.

The same transaction structures used for jpegs today could power better wallets, custody systems, contracts, or financial applications tomorrow.

So the real fight is should Bitcoin change its core rules to block a use case that already follows the rules and pays fees?

The rollout has problems of its own.

Back in February, a Bitcoin Core dev found a consensus bug in the activation software. A running node could accept a block that a freshly synced BIP-110 node would reject, leaving them on different versions of Bitcoin's history.

It got patched, and a later audit called the fix clean. But it shows why consensus changes need far more testing than a normal software update.

As of late June, 0.31% of hashrate was signaling support. The threshold is 55%. F2Pool has publicly refused, and no other major pool has joined.

The real deadline is early August. Around block 961,632, mandatory signaling kicks in and BIP-110 nodes start rejecting every block that doesn't signal support. At current levels, that's nearly every block.

I’m watching  whether those nodes end up on their own minority chain or their operators quietly back down.

IMO cutting useless data is a fair goal. But a consensus change is the strongest tool Bitcoin has, and without clear evidence of serious harm, the fix creates more risk than the problem it solves.

#2 - BIP-361

This is an early plan for protecting Bitcoin from future quantum computers.

It would introduce safer wallet types, give users roughly five years to move their $BTC, and later stop old vulnerable addresses from receiving or spending coins.

Nothing changes today bc quantum computers can't currently break Bitcoin.

But wallet migrations take years, and Bitcoin moves slowly by design.

The point is to lock the rules in before the threat becomes real, especially for old wallets whose public keys are already visible and for dormant coins whose owners may never return.

There are two diff fights happening but the question is how high should the bar be for changing Bitcoin's rules?

thread_tweets.text:
🍁With trade tensions heating back up — Trump has vowed a fresh 50% tariff on some Canadian goods, invoking Section 338 of the 1930 Tariff Act (a Depression-era provision never before used for tariffs) to hit ~$20B of imports like milk, beer, hockey equipment and plywood, while sparing energy, potash and critical minerals — here's what the data is actually saying:

☑️  Canada's effective tariff rate sits near 3.1% — below Mexico's ~3.7%. Yet Canada's imports stalled through the 2025 escalation while Mexico's climbed to a record ~$55B in May.

☑️  Since 2016, Mexico is up ~140% vs Canada's ~63%. They started roughly equal — Mexico has pulled decisively ahead.

☑️  Why? Canada's headline number is dominated by energy, which moves on price, not policy. Strip it out and the tariffs are clearly biting where they're aimed: steel & aluminum down ~40% since 2022 ($3.9B→$2.4B), autos sliding (as low as $2.6B in Jan), lumber halved.

☑️  Meanwhile Canada has no nearshoring engine — machinery/electronics is flat (~$3.5B), the exact category booming for Mexico.

Bottom line: Mexico is winning nearshoring; Canada is absorbing tariffs on metals and autos — with nothing structural to offset it. Same trade war, opposite trajectories.

tweets.text: One pattern I find useful for working with LLMs is a nice long ramble session. Sometimes the LLM needs more bits to understand what you're trying to achieve, but you're too lazy to type them. In these cases I like to lean back, switch to /voice and just ramble for like 10 minutes, total mess, anything goes, full stream of consciousness. Sometimes I declare it up top, something like "switching to speech recognition sorry for any typos...". Sometimes I turn it into a small interview of a few turns. But I find that the LLMs are somehow very good at reconstructing long incoherent rambles and often their echo of your own tangle of thoughts comes out quite a bit cleaner than what you started with. The result is that you improve the mind meld and have to correct things less from that point on.

tweets.text:
A new type of "high-level programming language" that seems really worth trying to make, is a language that gets compiled to Lean (or HOL, or...) that is specifically about making it as friendly as possible for a human to read definitions and theorems.

Not the proofs - as all that matters with proofs is that the proofs are correct - just the definitions and theorems.

The intended use case is that AI outputs a blob of proofs, and you're trying to make it as easy as possible for anyone reading the output to understand what the actual precise claims are that have been proven

tweets.text:
$ZIM: Trading With Handcuffs On

Every day $ZIM buyout process continues, shareholder value is not being fully maximized.

Not because the business is weak. Quite the opposite.

The current freight market has turned $ZIM into a money-printing machine. Yet management remains constrained while the buyout process is unresolved.

A company that could be returning more capital to shareholders, paying special dividends, optimizing its fleet, and monetizing non-core assets is instead limited by the ongoing process.

Take Matson ( $MATX ) as an example.

Matson is widely considered one of the highest-quality shipping companies in the industry. It benefits from the protected Jones Act, which limits foreign competition on domestic U.S. shipping routes, giving it stable earnings and premium valuation multiples.

It is also currently aggressively buying back its own stock, something $ZIM cannot do as long as the buyout process drags on.

https://t.co/zCkIOhgItE

Now let’s compare the two companies.

$MATX
• Market cap: ~$6.7B
• Cash: ~$119M
• Fleet: ~68,000 TEU
• Expected annual earnings: ~$400M

$ZIM
• Market cap: ~$3.0B
• Cash today: ~$2.6B
• Our estimates: ~$3.0B cash by the end of Q2 and ~$3.8 by Q4.
• Fleet: ~720,000 TEU, growing toward ~900,000 TEU
• Expected annual earnings: ~$700M–1.5B

Think about that for a moment.

The market values $MATX at more than twice the market capitalization of $ZIM.

Yet $ZIM has:
More than 10x the fleet capacity.
Cash equal to almost its entire market capitalization today.
The potential, in our view, to earn 2–4x as much as $MATX under the current freight environment.

Matson deserves a premium. It is an outstanding business.

But does it deserve to be worth more than twice a company that is dramatically larger, has billions more in cash, and could generate substantially higher earnings?

We don’t think so.

One final point.

In our view, the probability of $ZIM ultimately being sold, whether to an Israeli buyer or a foreign buyer, is close to zero.

Yet the ongoing buyout process continues to prevent management from fully maximizing shareholder value through capital allocation decisions such as special dividends, fleet optimization, and strategic asset sales.

The sooner this overhang is removed, the sooner management can focus entirely on unlocking the value that we believe is already there.

One important clarification: this is a long-term investment. If you’re looking for a quick 20% gain over the next few months, this is probably not the right stock.

In fact, the share price could still decline when the merger is officially terminated. There may still be investors holding on to the belief that the deal could somehow go through, even though we believe the market has largely recognized that the acquisition is no longer likely.

Our investment horizon is at least two years. As we mentioned before, we believe investors could receive at least a 50% total return from dividends alone over that period, assuming our outlook proves correct. The amount of capital appreciation is much harder to estimate, so we won’t speculate on it.

This is not a momentum trade. It is a long-term investment based on what we believe is a significant disconnect between the company’s intrinsic value and its current market price.

tweets.text:
Why Is $STAA Falling?

In my opinion, the recent weakness has little to do with the company’s underlying business.

The company preannounced an excellent quarter, with expected Q2 revenue of more than $90 million versus $44.3 million a year ago. China continued to recover, inventory levels normalized, and the Americas posted double digit growth.

So why is the stock down?

I think there are three reasons.

1. Higher interest rate fears.

Growth stocks have been under pressure as investors worry that interest rates could stay higher for longer.

2. Management’s cautious comments.

Although the preliminary results were strong, management warned that the Middle East and other regions affected by war, geopolitical instability, and economic uncertainty could continue to weigh on results.

3. Concerns about China competition.

Wedbush hosted an advisor call with a former STAAR Surgical Senior Vice President to discuss the China recovery and the threat from local competitors, especially EyeBright.

The discussion suggested that domestic competitors could gain meaningful unit share over the next two years. At the same time, the former executive emphasized that STAAR still has a significant moat through its proprietary Collamer technology, long clinical track record, and strong brand.

To me, none of these points change the long term thesis.

In fact, the business today is significantly stronger than it was when Alcon offered $30.75 per share near the bottom of the cycle. Since then, quarterly revenue has more than doubled from the depressed Q2 2025 level, yet the stock trades well below that offer.

I continue to believe STAAR is an attractive acquisition candidate. At today’s valuation, I think the risk reward is compelling.

As always, I never tell people when to sell a stock. When you’re up 100%, taking some profits is usually a reasonable decision. 

Comment: I doubled my position yesterday.

tweets.text:
𝙏𝙝𝙚 𝘾𝙖𝙪𝙨𝙚 𝙔𝙤𝙪 𝙉𝙚𝙫𝙚𝙧 𝙎𝙖𝙬

One of the central insights of Buddhism is that every event arises from countless causes and conditions. Most of them are impossible for us to see, let alone predict.

Imagine this.

A cold front over the Pacific changes the jet stream. Three days later, Seoul has perfect weather.

Because the weather is so nice, Jung Min-ho, one of the leaders of the Korean Ants, decides to go clubbing instead of staying home. He gets home at 4 a.m., wakes up in a surprisingly good mood, and messages his friends:

“Today we buy on leverage.”

The Korean market closes up 5%.

Nasdaq futures follow another 1.5%.

A few hours later, Wall Street publishes detailed reports explaining that the rally was caused by macroeconomic data, interest rate expectations, and improving sentiment.

Maybe those explanations contain part of the truth. But maybe the chain of causes began with a change in the weather over the Pacific. Or perhaps even earlier, with causes nobody could ever identify.

The Buddhist point is not that events happen randomly. It’s that reality is shaped by an unimaginably vast web of causes and conditions. We see only a tiny fraction of that web, yet our minds confidently invent simple stories that make us feel as if we understand why things happened.

tweets.text:
Marco Zappacosta (@mlz) went to school with my daughter Jesse.

His dad is Pierluigi Zappacosta, the founder of Logitech, so Marco grew up watching what it looks like to build something from scratch.

When he pitched me @thumbtack, I made him an offer almost on the spot because of the quiet confidence he wields. You can feel it in a room when someone genuinely sees a problem that shouldn't exist and has the energy and the intelligence to make it right.

His insight was simple: there was no real marketplace connecting the millions of plumbers, cleaners, movers, and contractors who power homes and offices with the people who actually need them. Every other marketplace was either too narrow or too broken.

He fixed that.

The company has redefined how homeowners and landlords do home improvement and repair.. The company is still private, still growing revenues, still processing over millions of customer service requests a year across millions of registered users.

That quiet confidence in the pitch room showed up in how he ran the company too. It looks like a great success.

tweets.text:
i don't think Ethereum is commie (though there are commies who like it), but real talk:

Ethereum's branding over the past 5-10 years has held it back and made it unattractive to many audiences who SHOULD use it

an overt focus on "magic and whimsy" (by the EF and others) rather than on hardened public architecture harmed the public perception (and possibly adoption) of Ethereum

which is a shame because in many ways the core of Ethereum is much harder than anything else in programmable crypto

but there are now new institutional players in the space though with serious exposure to Ethereum who will want to appeal to those audiences directly

it's time to shift the focus from "unicorns and rainbows" to "security which exceeds the guarantees provided by even nation-states"

seeing signs of that shift already, even at the EF
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Ethereum should ditch the communist egalitarian/child's eighth birthday unicorn branding and code Machiavellian 

That is far more the point of decentralized networks... resistance to adversaries, and brutal respect for the explicitly non-egalitarian state of both nature and man

> the funny thing about decentralization, is that community uprisings are the whole point

agree to an extent, but also disagree. uprisings reflect that the people in power are disconnected from the desires of the plebs

so what it frames here is a big disconnect between the people who control(led) the R&D and the hard fork processes and the majority of the users

uprising is not inherently the point of decentralization. ideally, there should be nothing to uprise against

imo EF will be most successful in the new environment not by doubling down on hippie culture, but rather evolving further alongside Ethereum

saw some of this when Tomasz stepped in and now more recently in direct discussion around ETH value accrual. good moves, but also probably several years late

but i think there's also a chance EF doubles down and becomes more hippie than ever because they see it as their mission in life in an environment otherwise devoid of hippies and they feel that influence is needed now more than ever

idk. the people working there are gonna have to decide

personally, i think going more stealth in a way and focusing on hard mission stuff would be the better approach. become like a "Second Foundation" which exists in the back ground to backstop permissionless, censorship-resistance, etc. in case those things ever come under threat

tweets.text:
Silicon Valley business lifecycle:

- build new thing with a very small number of people, designed to be used by a very large number of people
- subsidize access to it until it kills any existing longstanding competitors in similar / adjacent markets
- raise prices as much as possible based upon your effective monopoly (or very low competition space)
- use regulatory capture to prevent new competitors from entering the market
- turn into a lending and payments app (optional)

the only problem is it doesn't work if someone is willing to come in and give away a better product with no additional rent charged on top

talk to any real person and they'll tell you most of these products have become shittier, provide less value than they provided before, have become laden with dark patterns designed to trick users into upsells they don't want, while corporate profits are higher than ever

most businesses outside of tech cannot / do not operate like this

thread_tweets.text:
The best misinformation campaigns require a sliver of truth (the seed), a hot topic (sports, racism, immigration, war), and a spark. Then they grow like wildfire. Although not a requirement, they can be strengthened by using KOLs and bot armies. 

Your last question assumes trending volume equals incident volume. It doesn't. 48 fanbases produce plenty of ugly moments; the question is whose clips get selected and pushed. 

It only takes one spark, even a random one. Who may benefit? Engagement farms for sure. Ronaldo-aligned fan ecosystems. Madridistas. Rival fanbase media. Brands backing Messi's rivals. Even states: Milei's embrace of Israel makes Argentina a target for Iran linked networks.

Some incidents are real and worth condemning. Extrapolating them to 46 million people is exactly how these campaigns work.

You can read about the illusory truth effect below: mere repetition makes claims feel true and get shared.

tweets.text:
Man... AI is cheap but it's so expensive
​
Cost to get started (inference per 1M token) went from $18.40 to $6.07 (3x cheaper in a year)
​
But the cost to continuously run it (agentic workflows) is 23.4x more expensive
​
On top of this, more users are adopting AI, and each user is using AI to do more — same engineer who used Claude only for code review last year is now using it to code, book his flights, manage his personal finance, get him a gf, and be his best man on his wedding day
​
And the cheap but expensive problem is getting worse...
​
I've seen lots of AI app builders use models like DeepSeek v4 Flash & Pro (because they're cheap) but DeepSeek just hiked up their prices 2x during peak China hours
​
Even if you shift to other providers, say Kimi, you can't because they're running out of compute to serve new users (you still can't subscribe rn, it's all sold out
​
Feels like strategies that optimize your token spend would get a lot more important from here (e.g. orchestrator + executor pipeline, AI observability tools, better loops & graphs, decentralized inference if/when that becomes ready)
​
Whelp.... for the time being, more dough for inference players I guess...
​
(My Hermes monthly spend about to change from $40-50/month to $80-100, gotta figure out how to optimize this)
